description:
You are assisting a data-science team that wants to measure, at a very high level, how long it takes to “clean” a small CSV file that contains a few missing values.  
Your goal is to:  

1. Copy the file /home/user/datasets/raw/sales.csv to your working directory (or read it from its current location).  
2. Produce a cleaned version of the file that contains **only** rows whose three fields (id, region, sales) are all non-empty.  
   • Save the cleaned file as /home/user/datasets/clean/sales_clean.csv.  
3. Benchmark the wall-clock time, user CPU time, and system CPU time required for the cleaning command by wrapping the cleaning command in “/usr/bin/time -p”.  
4. Capture both the timing statistics and a short summary of the dataset sizes (in lines) before and after cleaning in a single log file named /home/user/benchmark/cleaning_benchmark.log.

VERY IMPORTANT – the log file must have exactly the following structure so that automated tests can parse it.  Use the literal section separators, keywords, and punctuation shown below; only the numeric values are allowed to change:

Benchmark Report
================
Command: clean_sales
real:<real_time_seconds>
user:<user_time_seconds>
sys:<sys_time_seconds>
Lines_raw:6
Lines_clean:4

Notes on the required format:  
• Every line shown above (including the row of = signs) must appear exactly once and in exactly that order.  
• Do NOT put extra spaces before or after the colons.  
• Replace the “\<real_time_seconds\>”, “\<user_time_seconds\>”, and “\<sys_time_seconds\>” placeholders with the numeric values that /usr/bin/time -p prints for the cleaning command.  Seconds may be integers or decimals.  
• The automated checker will ignore anything after the sixth decimal place, so don’t worry about precision.  
• “Lines_raw” must report the line count of the original file (header + data rows).  
• “Lines_clean” must report the line count of the cleaned file (header + data rows).  

A correct solution therefore creates two new artefacts:

1. /home/user/datasets/clean/sales_clean.csv — the cleaned dataset.  
2. /home/user/benchmark/cleaning_benchmark.log — the log, strictly following the format above.

Only basic POSIX utilities (cp, awk, grep, wc, /usr/bin/time, etc.) are necessary; no Python or external libraries are required.  Fewer than four terminal commands are enough to accomplish everything if you combine steps carefully.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (already present before the agent starts):

Directory: /home/user/datasets/raw  
File: /home/user/datasets/raw/sales.csv  
Exact byte-for-byte contents of sales.csv (LF line endings):

id,region,sales
1,North,100
2,South,200
3,,150
4,East,
5,West,300

(The file therefore contains exactly 6 lines.)

Directories that the agent must create (if they do not already exist):  
• /home/user/datasets/clean  
• /home/user/benchmark

Artefacts that must exist when the agent finishes:

1. /home/user/datasets/clean/sales_clean.csv  
   Expected contents (exact; LF endings):

id,region,sales
1,North,100
2,South,200
5,West,300

   The file has exactly 4 lines.

2. /home/user/benchmark/cleaning_benchmark.log  
   Must match this regular-expression template (POSIX BRE):

^Benchmark Report\n=+\nCommand: clean_sales\nreal:[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?\nuser:[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?\nsys:[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?\nLines_raw:6\nLines_clean:4\n?$

The automated test will:
• Verify that the cleaned CSV exactly matches the contents listed above.  
• Verify that the log file exists, is world-readable, and matches the regex template (the time values may vary).  
• Verify that the numeric values for Lines_raw and Lines_clean are exactly “6” and “4”, respectively.

No other files or directories are required.
